title: The ‘Mission: Impossible’ Mailbag, and Movie Rankings
format: Sean and Amanda bring on producer Bobby Wagner for a strike-week Mission: Impossible mailbag, then rank all seven films in the franchise.

segments.heading: The SAG-AFTRA Strike and the Fall Festival Season

films.title: Far and Away
films.note: Cited as Cruise trivia: he trained to box for Ron Howard's period drama with soon-to-be wife Nicole Kidman, long before the stunt era.

films.title: Days of Thunder
films.note: Brought up while debating whether Cruise has ever personally dialed a phone, maybe to discuss dailies with John C. Reilly.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: Luca Guadagnino's Zendaya tennis drama, set to launch Venice; discussed as a film that needs her red-carpet star power to sell a mid-tier sports drama.

films.title: Don't Worry Darling
films.note: Held up as a contrast: its off-screen drama drew attention at Venice last year even though the film itself wasn't beloved.

films.title: The Holdovers
films.note: Alexander Payne's new one; flagged as a film that will lean on Paul Giamatti to sell it if star promotion stays frozen.

films.title: Barbie
films.note: Used as the case study for how much a press tour matters: the Margot Robbie and Ryan Gosling blitz was called inescapable.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: Sean is headed to a screening as they record; discussed alongside Barbie as a test of legs without full star-driven marketing.

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: The mailbag's spine: whether the franchise survives Cruise's eventual exit, who could plausibly replace him (no one, they conclude), the Gabriel/entity plot, which stunt they'd actually attempt, the best villain, and the Ilsa Faust death fans are still mad about.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Referenced repeatedly as the ceiling. Cruise is 'still Cruise, but not the Cruise of Maverick, because no one is.'

films.title: Extraction 2
films.note: Chris Hemsworth's 'po-faced' action turn, cited while debating whether he could plug into an Ethan Hunt-style role.

films.title: Ballerina
films.note: The John Wick spinoff with Ana de Armas, used as the closest comp. Amanda says she's showing up for Keanu, not the franchise.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 4
films.note: Its exploding gun becomes the punchline for a Mets bullpen collapse.

films.title: X-Men: First Class
films.note: The model for recasting a franchise around one or two familiar faces in a period setting.

films.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
films.note: Cited as a cautionary tale for how hard it is to keep a franchise's tone while writing around an absent lead.

films.title: Casablanca
films.note: The joke answer to 'bring back any character': Rick, for Dead Reckoning Part Two.

films.title: EverAfter
films.note: Dougray Scott's other credit, raised after his what-if casting as Wolverine comes up.

films.title: Green Lantern
films.note: The bar The Flash is failing to clear: even Ryan Reynolds' version out-grossed it domestically.

films.title: The Flash
films.note: Cited as the summer's box-office disappointment.

films.title: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny
films.note: Used to illustrate how tepid reception shortens a film's theatrical life.

films.title: Elemental
films.note: Called the summer's most interesting box-office story for its unusually small weekly drops.

films.title: Insidious: The Red Door
films.note: Its steep opening-weekend drop is contrasted with Elemental's staying power.

films.title: Casino Royale
films.note: Vesper Lynd's train scene with Bond is pointed out as the direct model for Ethan and Grace's Italian-prison speech.

films.title: Doctor Sleep
films.note: Rebecca Ferguson's other big performance, brought up when Amanda mixes up its hat with Ilsa's death scene.

films.title: Eyes Wide Shut
films.note: Cited as part of Cruise's 'strange era': filming for eight years while his marriage ended.

films.title: Vanilla Sky
films.note: Named in the same breath as Eyes Wide Shut as Cruise's pre-Woo detour.

films.title: Dr. No
films.note: Invoked to argue MI original vs. Fallout isn't like picking the first Bond over Skyfall: the original stands fully on its own.

films.title: Skyfall
films.note: The other half of that Bond comparison.

segments.heading: The Mission: Impossible Rankings
segments.note: all seven films, best to worst
segments.subject: Mission: Impossible

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Fallout
films.note: No. 1 (2-1 vote): Amanda and Bobby call it 'the peak of the form.'

films.title: Mission: Impossible
films.note: No. 2: Sean's pick for the top spot and, he says, the 'correct letterboxd answer.' De Palma's template-setter, tube heist and all.

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Rogue Nation
films.note: No. 3: wins out over Dead Reckoning on the strength of the opera sequence and Solomon Lane's introduction.

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: No. 4: the Rome car chase is praised, but the opera-house showdown edges it out for two of the three.

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Ghost Protocol
films.note: No. 5: the Burj Khalifa climb 'sets the mold' even if the CGI has aged; the Kremlin break-in gets a late shout-out.

films.title: Mission: Impossible III
films.note: No. 6: praised for Philip Seymour Hoffman's villain and the Vatican heist, docked for a visual style that 'looks like TV.'

films.title: Mission: Impossible II
films.note: Last: John Woo's maximalism, they agree, was never a fit for the series.

films: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly

Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Par.title: Mission: Impossible - Dead Reckoning Part One

Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Che.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest

Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny.title: Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny

The Good, the Bad and the Ugly.title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
